chunk_summary: Responsive feeding emphasizes recognizing hunger and satiety cues, avoiding coercion, and fostering structured, calm mealtime practices to support healthy growth and appetite regulation in infants and toddlers aged 0–24 months.
learning_objectives: Identify hunger and satiety cues in infants and toddlers during feeding.
Understand the importance of non-coercive feeding practices for healthy appetite regulation.
Implement structured mealtime routines to promote optimal nutrition and reduce feeding conflicts.
Recognize the role of responsive feeding in addressing stunting, anemia, and overweight in specific populations.
key_facts: Hunger cues include rooting, hand-to-mouth movements, leaning forward, and excitement at mealtime.
Satiety cues include slowing pace, turning away, spitting out food, or pushing the spoon away.
Meals should ideally last 20–30 minutes; prolonged feeding conflicts should be avoided.
Positioning the child upright and maintaining eye contact during feeding supports safe swallowing.
In India, responsive feeding is critical due to the coexistence of stunting, anemia, and rising overweight rates.
Limiting distractions like screens during meals helps children sense fullness and prevents overfeeding.
reference_quote: Responsive feeding supports healthy appetite regulation while permitting therapeutic 'energy boosting' when growth falters. (WHO Guiding Principles for Complementary Feeding of the Breastfed Child)
document_key_topics: Responsive feeding practices for infants and toddlers
Hunger and satiety cues during feeding
